1.pericope_label: Jesus Heals a Man Born Blind
1.synthesis: Jesus passes a man who has been blind from birth, and when the disciples ask whose sin caused his condition, Jesus refuses the retribution framework entirely: the man's blindness exists "so that the works of God might be displayed in him" (v.3). With daylight running — "the night is coming, when no one can work" (v.4) — Jesus makes mud from saliva and earth, applies it, and sends the man to wash in the Pool of Siloam. The man goes, washes, and comes back seeing (v.7). Neighbours who had known him as a beggar argue about whether this is the same man; he insists, "I am the man" (v.9), and explains simply: "The man called Jesus made mud and anointed my eyes and said to me, 'Go to Siloam and wash.' So I went and washed and received my sight" (v.11). He does not yet know where Jesus is; he obeyed a command before he met the one who gave it.
1.historical_context: Blind beggars stationed at temple precincts were a fixture of ancient urban life; charitable giving to the disabled was a religious duty, and pilgrimage traffic made the temple area the most productive location. The disciples' question — "Who sinned, this man or his parents?" (v.2) — reflects a live Second Temple debate: Exodus 20:5 threatened visitation of iniquity across generations, and some rabbinic opinions even debated whether a child could sin in the womb. The Pharisees' later taunt — "you were born entirely in sins" (v.34) — shows this reading was weaponized. Jesus' clay-making on the Sabbath (v.14) violated two distinct Mishnaic prohibitions (kneading and applying saliva to eyes on the Sabbath, m. Shabb. 7:2; 14:4), making the healing itself a provocation. The Pool of Siloam was fed by Hezekiah's tunnel and bore the Tabernacles significance: priests drew water from it daily during the feast for the libation ceremony — its Evangelist's gloss, "which means Sent" (v.7), activates the name shiloach as a pointer to Jesus, the one sent by the Father.
1.literary_structure: The chapter is architecturally unified around the trial of testimony. The blind man's arc of confession — "the man called Jesus" (v.11), "he is a prophet" (v.17), "Lord, I believe" and worship (v.38) — traces the spiritual journey from physical sight to faith while the Pharisees move in the reverse direction: from seeing the evidence to willful blindness. The opening pericope sets the motif: "I am the light of the world" (v.5) announces what the healing will dramatize. "Night is coming" (v.4) sets the eschatological urgency, and the pool's name binds the physical washing to the mission of the Sent One. The man's neighbours' debate — "It is he" / "No, he only looks like him" (vv.8–9) — is the chapter's comic-ironic opening: those who had seen him daily cannot recognize the man who can now see.
1.theological_application: Jesus' refusal to embed the man's suffering in a retribution grid is one of the Gospel's most humanizing moments. The question "who sinned?" makes the sufferer a theological puzzle to be solved; Jesus makes him a person in whom God's glory will be displayed. This is not a denial that sin has consequences, but a refusal to make a specific man's specific blindness the occasion for a theology of divine punishment. The healing enacts the promise of Isaiah 35:5 — in the messianic age, the eyes of the blind will be opened — and places the one standing before the disciples within the calendar of redemptive history: the works of God are being displayed now, in him, through the one who is the light. The man's simple obedience — going and washing without having seen or known his healer — is the form faith takes when the word precedes the sight.
1.christology: "I am the light of the world" (v.5) echoes John 8:12 and the Tabernacles lamp ceremony the discourse has just addressed; now the claim becomes an acted sign. The one who announces himself as light gives sight to a man who has never seen light. The mud-and-saliva method, deliberately provocative in its Sabbath violations, signals that the Creator is not constrained by the law's sabbatical fences: the same Lord who formed Adam from the dust (Genesis 2:7) re-creates a defective eye from clay. The Pool of Siloam — the Sent One — is the locus of the miracle: those who go to where Jesus directs them receive sight; those who refuse to go remain blind. The light has come, and the choice is whether to walk in it.

13.synthesis: The healed man is brought to the Pharisees, who begin a formal Sabbath inquiry. He tells his story again; they split over it (v.16). Asked his opinion, he calls Jesus a prophet (v.17). The authorities disbelieve the blindness itself and summon his parents, who confirm both the birth-blindness and the present sight but refuse to say how, fearing expulsion from the synagogue (vv.21–22). The man is summoned again and ordered to give glory to God by admitting Jesus is a sinner (v.24). His answer is the chapter's centerpiece: "Whether he is a sinner I do not know. One thing I do know: I was blind, and now I see" (v.25). Pressed repeatedly, he turns the logic of his interrogators back on them — "God does not listen to sinners" (v.31); "if this man were not from God, he could do nothing" (v.33). They expel him with a curse: "You were born entirely in sins" (v.34).
13.historical_context: "They brought him to the Pharisees" (v.13) reflects the Pharisees' recognized authority over Sabbath adjudication; reporting a potential violation was a communal religious obligation. The tripled repetition of the question "how were your eyes opened?" (v.10, v.15, v.26) mirrors Mishnaic cross-examination procedure: witnesses were questioned separately and repeatedly to detect inconsistencies (m. Sanh. 4:1; 5:2). The oath formula "Give glory to God" (v.24) is drawn directly from Joshua 7:19, where Joshua urges Achan to confess before execution. The term aposynagōgos ("expelled from the synagogue," v.22) appears only in John (John 12:42; John 16:2) and likely reflects the formal social exclusion available to the synagogue community — a measure that carried economic, legal, and social weight far beyond religious inconvenience.
13.literary_structure: The trial scene has the structure of an ascending irony. The man who was born blind progressively sees more clearly while the Pharisees — who claim to see — become increasingly unable to process what is in front of them. "We are disciples of Moses" (v.28) against "you are this fellow's disciple" (v.28) frames the chapter's central contrast as two schools of authority. The man's logic at vv.30–33 — a careful deduction from shared Pharisaic premises to a conclusion they cannot accept — is presented as the most reasonable argument in the room, yet it produces expulsion. "You were born entirely in sins" (v.34) uses against him the very retribution theology Jesus had rejected at the chapter's opening (v.3): those who will not accept the verdict of the miracle reach for social exclusion instead.
13.theological_application: "One thing I do know: I was blind, and now I see" (v.25) is the charter of simple witness. The man cannot produce a Christology; he cannot resolve the Sabbath question; he cannot argue the Pharisees into faith. What he can do is state, with unassailable directness, what was true before and what is true now. This form of testimony — grounded in experienced transformation rather than theoretical elaboration — is proof against any cross-examination that cannot deny the change itself. The parents' fearful silence is the contrasting case: they know what they know but will not say it, and their silence is the silence of every generation that lets institutional fear override personal witness. Expulsion for honesty is the cost of truthful testimony; the man pays it and is, by that act, ready for the next scene.
13.christology: The Pharisees' invocation of Moses as their master — "we know that God spoke to Moses, but as for this man, we do not know where he comes from" (v.29) — is the Gospel's irony at full stretch: Moses himself wrote of Jesus (John 5:46), and God's speaking through Moses was always oriented toward this one. "Nobody has ever heard of opening the eyes of a man born blind" (v.32) is a claim that points directly at the prophetic texts (Isaiah 29:18; Isaiah 35:5; Isaiah 42:7) associating the recovery of sight with the messianic age. The unprecedented sign is precisely its credential; those who receive it as such move toward faith, while those who reject it are left — by their own insistence — in the dark.

35.pericope_label: Jesus Finds the Man and Speaks on Blindness
35.synthesis: Jesus hears that the man has been expelled and goes to find him — the seeking Shepherd already anticipating the discourse that follows. "Do you believe in the Son of Man?" (v.35). The man asks who that is so that he may believe, and Jesus answers: "You have seen him, and it is he who is speaking with you" (v.37). "Lord, I believe" — and he worships him (v.38). Then comes the verdict that frames the whole chapter: "For judgment I came into this world, that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind" (v.39). The Pharisees overhear and ask, "Are we also blind?" The answer is measured and final: if they were blind, they would have no guilt; but because they say "we see," their guilt remains (v.41).
35.historical_context: "Son of Man" in v.35 reaches back to Daniel 7:13–14, the heavenly figure who receives authority from the Ancient of Days and vindicates the saints against unjust oppressors. Its use here, immediately after an unjust expulsion, carries the connotation of divine vindication: the one who has been cast out by the institutional authorities is about to be found and vindicated by the figure with heavenly authority. The act of worship (prosekunēsen, v.38) is the same verb used throughout the Gospel for the worship due to God (John 4:20–24); to perform it toward Jesus is to attribute to him the status proper to deity. The concluding judgment saying at v.39 echoes Isaiah 6:9–10, which all four Gospels apply to the divided response to Jesus' ministry.
35.literary_structure: The scene is a structural mirror of the Prologue's light-darkness dynamic made fully visible in narrative. The man who could not see now sees, worships, and receives eternal life; the Pharisees who can see are declared blind. The chapter opened by refuting the sin-punishment framework (v.3); it closes by establishing a deeper accountability: the sin that remains is the claim to see combined with the refusal to see (v.41). Knowledge increases guilt; the light that saves is also the light that judges. The seeking and finding pattern — Jesus hearing of the expulsion and going to the man — anticipates chapter 10's portrait of the Good Shepherd who seeks the lost sheep, making this reunion the narrative bridge between the two discourses.
35.theological_application: The closing verdict is the Gospel's sharpest statement about the relationship between knowledge and culpability. To claim sight — to say "we see," to position oneself as an authoritative interpreter of the divine will — while refusing to acknowledge the evidence of God's work is not a neutral intellectual stance. It is guilt-constituting: "your sin remains" (v.41). Genuine blindness, if it could be complete, would remove the ground of condemnation; but no one who has heard the gospel is ever entirely blind in this sense. The light has come, and the question before each hearer is whether they will say "I was blind but now I see" or insist, against the evidence, that they already see and need no healer.
35.christology: "You have seen him, and it is he who is speaking with you" (v.37) echoes the Samaritan revelation (John 4:26) and the resurrection appearances where Christ makes himself known in speech. The gift of sight and the self-disclosure are inseparable: the man was made to see so that he might, in the end, see the Son of Man and believe. Christ's coming is "for judgment" (v.39) not in the sense that condemnation is his purpose — "I did not come to judge the world but to save it" (John 12:47) — but in the sense that his presence inevitably divides: the light that illuminates also exposes. The expelled beggar, worshiping at Christ's feet, is the chapter's icon of the gospel: cast out by the institution, found by the Shepherd, and receiving sight in every dimension.
